content:
# Galatians Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.1 — "caught in some trespass" vs. "overcome by some sin" — and restoration language

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if a man is caught in some trespass, you the spiritual [ones] restore such a one" | prolambanō — "caught/overtaken"; katartizete — "restore/mend" |
| LOGOS | "if anyone is caught in any transgression, you who are spiritual should restore him" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "if another believer is overcome by some sin, you who are godly should gently and humbly help that person back onto the right path" | "Overcome"; "godly" for pneumatikoi; "help...back onto the right path" for katartizete |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA changes "the spiritual ones" (pneumatikoi) to "godly" — losing the direct connection to pneuma/Spirit from chapter 5. PNEUMA also expands katartizete ("restore/mend") into "gently and humbly help that person back onto the right path" — adding "humbly" and restructuring.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses pneumatikoi connection and expands restoration.

---

### v.3 — "he deceives himself" vs. "you are only fooling yourself. You are not that important"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone thinks himself to be something, being nothing, he deceives himself" | phrenapataō — "deceive the mind" |
| LOGOS | "if anyone thinks he is something, when he is nothing, he deceives himself" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "If you think you are too important to help someone, you are only fooling yourself. You are not that important" | Adds context "too important to help"; "not that important" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul states a general principle about self-deception. PNEUMA adds "too important to help someone" — connecting it to v.1 but adding specificity not in the Greek. "You are not that important" is an editorial addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds interpretive context.

---

### v.5 — "his own load" vs. "our own conduct"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "each [one] will bear his own load" | phortion — "load/pack" |
| LOGOS | "each will have to bear his own load" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "we are each responsible for our own conduct" | "Conduct" for phortion; loses military/load metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. phortion means "load/pack" — a military or travel metaphor for personal responsibility. PNEUMA replaces the concrete image with the abstract "conduct," losing the distinction between baros (v.2, heavy burden) and phortion (v.5, personal load) that resolves the apparent contradiction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses the baros/phortion distinction.

---

### v.7 — "God is not mocked" vs. "you cannot mock the justice of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God is not mocked" | theos ou myktērizetai — "God is not sneered at" |
| LOGOS | "God is not mocked" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "you cannot mock the justice of God" | Adds "the justice of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "God is not mocked" — full stop. PNEUMA adds "the justice of," which narrows the statement to God's justice when Paul means God's entire person is not to be mocked.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "justice."

---

### v.8 — "corruption" vs. "decay and death" and sarx rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will reap from the flesh corruption" | phthoran — "corruption/decay/destruction" |
| LOGOS | "will from the flesh reap corruption" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "will harvest decay and death from that sinful nature" | "Decay and death" — doubles; "sinful nature" for sarx again |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. phthora means "corruption/destruction" — a single term. PNEUMA doubles it to "decay and death" and continues the "sinful nature" rendering of sarx from chapter 5.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA doubles and continues sarx = "sinful nature."

---

### v.10 — "household of the faith" vs. "family of faith"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] of the household of the faith" | tous oikeious tēs pisteōs — "members of the household of faith" |
| LOGOS | "those who are of the household of faith" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "those in the family of faith" | "Family" for oikeious |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. oikeios means "member of a household" — a term with economic and social overtones (the ancient household included extended family, slaves, and clients). "Household" preserves this. PNEUMA's "family" is a subset of the household concept.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — "household" vs. "family."

---

### v.12 — "make a good appearance in the flesh" vs. "look good to others" and motivation

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "As many as want to make a good appearance in [the] flesh" | euprosōpēsai en sarki — "make a fair face in flesh" |
| LOGOS | "those who want to make a good showing in the flesh" | "Good showing" — same |
| PNEUMA | "Those who are trying to force you to be circumcised want to look good to others. They don't want to be persecuted for teaching that the cross of Christ alone can save" | Splits and expands; adds "alone can save" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "alone can save" — Paul says they avoid persecution "for the cross of Christ," not "for teaching that the cross of Christ alone can save." The addition imports a soteriology statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds soteriological claim.

---

### v.14 — "through whom/which" and reciprocal crucifixion

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "through whom the world has been crucified to me and I to the world" | di' hou — "through whom" (referring to Christ) |
| LOGOS | "by which the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world" | di' hou — "by which" (referring to the cross) |
| PNEUMA | "Because of that cross, my interest in this world has been crucified, and the world's interest in me has also died" | "My interest...the world's interest" — psychologizes; adds "also died" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. di' hou could refer to "the cross" or "Christ" — both are grammatically possible. LOGOS takes "the cross" as antecedent. PNEUMA psychologizes the reciprocal crucifixion — "my interest in this world" and "the world's interest in me" reduces a cosmic event to subjective attitudes. Paul means the world-system and Paul are mutually dead, not merely uninterested.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. PNEUMA psychologizes the reciprocal crucifixion. Paul's statement is ontological (world and Paul are dead to each other), not psychological (they lost interest in each other). The difference affects whether the cross changes reality or merely changes perspective.

---

### v.15 — "a new creation" vs. "transformed into a new creation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "neither circumcision is anything nor uncircumcision, but a new creation" | kainē ktisis — "new creation" |
| LOGOS | "neither circumcision counts for anything, nor uncircumcision, but a new creation" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "It doesn't matter whether we have been circumcised or not. What counts is whether we have been transformed into a new creation" | "Transformed into" — process language; "whether we have been" — conditional |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's statement is declarative: "a new creation" — not "whether we have been transformed into a new creation." PNEUMA adds conditionality ("whether") and process language ("transformed into"), turning Paul's cosmic declaration into a conditional personal transformation.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "A new creation" (cosmic declaration) vs. "transformed into a new creation" (conditional personal process). Paul declares a new cosmic reality; PNEUMA makes it contingent on individual transformation.

---

### v.16 — "the Israel of God" — identification

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "peace upon them and mercy, and upon the Yisrael of God" | kai epi ton Israēl tou theou — preserves kai as additive |
| LOGOS | "peace and mercy be upon them, and upon the Israel of God" | Same — preserves ambiguity |
| PNEUMA | "they are the new people of God" | Identifies "Israel of God" as "the new people of God"; removes "Israel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The phrase "the Israel of God" is one of the most debated in Galatians. Does kai ("and") add a second group (ethnic Israel), or is it epexegetical ("that is, the Israel of God" = the church)? Both EMET and LOGOS preserve the ambiguity. PNEUMA eliminates both "Israel" and the ambiguity, rendering it as "the new people of God" — a supersessionist interpretation that Paul may or may not intend.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "The Israel of God" — ethnic Israel alongside the church? Or the church as the new Israel? PNEUMA's "new people of God" resolves a centuries-old ambiguity in a supersessionist direction. The consensus preserves the ambiguous "Israel of God."

---

### v.17 — "the marks of Yeshua" vs. "the marks of Jesus" vs. "the scars that show I belong to Jesus"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I bear on my body the marks of Yeshua" | ta stigmata tou Iēsou — "brand marks" |
| LOGOS | "I bear on my body the marks of Jesus" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I bear on my body the scars that show I belong to Jesus" | "Scars that show I belong to" — interpretive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. stigmata means "brand marks" — Paul's scars function as ownership marks. PNEUMA adds "that show I belong to" — an interpretive expansion that makes explicit what the metaphor implies. But Paul's statement is more compressed and more powerful in its brevity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA explicates the metaphor.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 5 | "Justice of," "alone can save," "too important to help," "new people of God," "scars that show I belong" |
| PNEUMA psychologizing | 1 | "My interest...the world's interest" for reciprocal crucifixion (v.14) |
| PNEUMA sarx = "sinful nature" | 1 | Continues from ch.5 (v.8) |
| "Israel of God" ambiguity | 1 | Ethnic Israel vs. church — PNEUMA eliminates ambiguity |
| "New creation" framing | 1 | Cosmic declaration vs. conditional personal transformation (v.15) |
| baros/phortion distinction | 1 | "Burden" vs. "load" — PNEUMA conflates (v.5) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 6 closes the letter with three major interpretive flashpoints: (1) the reciprocal crucifixion (v.14), where PNEUMA psychologizes an ontological statement; (2) "new creation" (v.15), where PNEUMA adds conditionality to a cosmic declaration; and (3) "the Israel of God" (v.16), where PNEUMA eliminates a centuries-old ambiguity in a supersessionist direction. These final-chapter choices carry enormous theological weight because they shape how readers understand the letter's ultimate claims about reality, identity, and the relationship between the church and Israel.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
